meta.title: SF-SPAA-4: Audit Logging, Compliance and Disaster Recovery

questions.stem: Which organization-level role can create new Snowflake accounts in an organization?

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN manages objects (databases, warehouses, schemas) within a single account — it has no organization-level privileges.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SECURITYADMIN manages security objects, grants, and network policies within an account but cannot create new accounts.

options.text: ORGADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. ORGADMIN is the only role that operates at the organization scope and can create new accounts within the organization using CREATE ACCOUNT.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. USERADMIN creates users and roles within an account; it has no organization-level capabilities.

questions.stem: Which command changes the active primary role for the current session?

options.text: SET ROLE
options.explanation: is incorrect. SET ROLE is not a valid Snowflake SQL command for changing the active session role.

options.text: ALTER SESSION SET ROLE
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER SESSION can modify session parameters such as TIMEZONE or QUERY_TAG, but it does not accept a ROLE clause for switching roles.

options.text: SWITCH ROLE
options.explanation: is incorrect. SWITCH ROLE is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

options.text: USE ROLE
options.explanation: is correct. USE ROLE activates a different primary role in the current session. Any subsequent SQL statements are evaluated against the privileges of the newly activated role.

questions.stem: A company wants separate billing statements and independent administration boundaries for two business units sharing the same Snowflake organization. What is the most appropriate design?

options.text: Two separate accounts within one organization
options.explanation: is correct. Separate accounts within the same organization give each business unit its own billing statement, its own admin roles, its own network policies, and its own governance boundary — while still allowing data sharing and cross-account usage views via ORGADMIN.

options.text: Two separate databases in one account with a resource monitor per database
options.explanation: is incorrect. Two databases in one account share the same billing account and the same administrative role hierarchy. Resource monitors track credit consumption but do not create separate billing invoices.

options.text: Two dedicated warehouses in one account tagged to separate cost centers
options.explanation: is incorrect. Separate warehouses with cost center tags can help allocate costs for showback, but the charges still appear on a single bill and both units share the same account-level admin roles and security settings.

options.text: One account with a custom role hierarchy per business unit
options.explanation: is incorrect. A custom role hierarchy per business unit provides logical access control separation but shares a single billing envelope and top-level ACCOUNTADMIN.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature provides centralized visibility into credit consumption, storage, and query activity across all accounts in an organization?

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE schema within each account
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE is scoped to a single account. Each account has its own ACCOUNT_USAGE schema, so it cannot provide a cross-account consolidated view without manual aggregation.

options.text: Reader account usage reports
options.explanation: is incorrect. Reader accounts are a data sharing mechanism for providing external consumers read-only access. They do not aggregate organizational usage metrics.

options.text: ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema in the shared SNOWFLAKE database
options.explanation: is correct. The SNOWFLAKE.ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema contains views accessible to ORGADMIN that aggregate credit, storage, and other usage metrics across every account in the organization, enabling centralized multi-account reporting.

options.text: Marketplace Analytics dashboard
options.explanation: is incorrect. Marketplace Analytics pertains to data product providers and consumers on Snowflake Marketplace; it is unrelated to cross-account operational usage reporting.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of a failover group in Snowflake?

options.text: To replicate account objects to a secondary account and enable promotion to primary for disaster recovery
options.explanation: is correct. A failover group replicates supported account objects — databases, warehouses, users, roles, network policies, and more — to one or more secondary accounts, and allows a secondary to be promoted to primary status to restore read-write operations during a regional outage.

options.text: To load files from an external stage into a target table
options.explanation: is incorrect. Loading files from an external stage is accomplished with the COPY INTO command and is unrelated to failover groups.

options.text: To apply dynamic data masking policies across shared databases
options.explanation: is incorrect. Dynamic data masking policies are object-level security policies applied to columns; they are part of access control, not failover groups. (Masking policies can be replicated as part of a failover group, but that is not their purpose.)

options.text: To enforce MFA enrollment for service account users
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA enrollment is configured per user through account authentication policies and is independent of failover groups.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to enforce a minimum idle session timeout of 30 minutes for all users in the account. Which mechanism is most appropriate?

options.text: Set the CLIENT_SESSION_KEEP_ALIVE account parameter to FALSE
options.explanation: is incorrect. CLIENT_SESSION_KEEP_ALIVE is a client-side parameter that prevents session timeouts in some drivers; it does not set a minimum timeout and cannot enforce a policy at the account level.

options.text: Create a session policy with an idle timeout value and attach it to the account
options.explanation: is correct. Session policies are Snowflake objects that define idle and maximum session timeout values. When attached at the account level they apply uniformly to all users, enforcing the required idle timeout.

options.text: Grant a resource monitor with a suspend action to all warehouses
options.explanation: is incorrect. A resource monitor with a suspend action monitors credit consumption and suspends warehouses when a credit threshold is reached — it does not control session timeouts.

options.text: Set AUTO_SUSPEND on each warehouse to 30 minutes
options.explanation: is incorrect. AUTO_SUSPEND controls how long a warehouse idles before its compute is shut down. It is a compute cost-saving setting, not a session security timeout.

questions.stem: Which built-in Snowflake role is specifically designed for creating and managing users and roles within an account?

options.text: PUBLIC
options.explanation: is incorrect. PUBLIC is the default role automatically granted to every user. It typically has no elevated privileges and is not used for user or role administration.

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN is responsible for creating and managing account-level data objects such as warehouses, databases, and schemas. It does not have CREATE USER by default.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. USERADMIN is the system-defined role whose primary purpose is creating and managing users and roles. It has the CREATE USER and CREATE ROLE system privileges.

options.text: ORGADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. ORGADMIN operates at the organization scope and manages account-level organization tasks. It is not the designated role for routine user and role creation within an account.

questions.stem: A company wants the strongest isolation between development and production environments — separate credentials, separate governance, and separate billing. Which pattern achieves this?

options.text: Separate warehouses tagged DEV and PROD within one account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Separate warehouses in one account split compute and can help with cost attribution via resource monitors, but credentials, roles, and governance settings are all still shared at the account level.

options.text: Separate databases within one account with schema-level access controls
options.explanation: is incorrect. Separate databases provide object namespacing and can support different role hierarchies, but they share the same ACCOUNTADMIN, the same network policies, and the same billing statement.

options.text: Separate accounts for dev and prod within the same organization
options.explanation: is correct. Separate accounts provide complete isolation: independent credentials, independent ACCOUNTADMIN hierarchies, independent network policies, and separate billing. Sharing data between them still works via Snowflake's cross-account data sharing.

options.text: Separate schemas within one database with future grants per environment
options.explanation: is incorrect. Schema separation with future grants provides logical access control boundaries but no billing or credential isolation — all schemas share a single account.

questions.stem: Why should ACCOUNTADMIN not be used as the primary role for day-to-day analyst work?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN cannot execute SELECT queries on tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN inherits all privileges in the account, including SELECT on any table. It can absolutely run SELECT queries.

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN automatically suspends warehouses after each query
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN has no behavior that auto-suspends warehouses after each query. Warehouse suspension is governed by the AUTO_SUSPEND setting on the warehouse itself.

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN holds broad account-level privileges that violate least-privilege principles if used routinely
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNTADMIN is the highest-privilege role in a Snowflake account — it can modify billing, drop databases, alter security settings, and override governance controls. Using it for routine analyst work means a compromised or mistaken query can cause account-wide damage, violating the principle of least privilege.

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN disables MFA for any session that uses it
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA is configured per user as an authentication policy. Using ACCOUNTADMIN as a session role does not disable MFA for that session.

questions.stem: Which two object types can be included in a Snowflake replication or failover group? (Select TWO)

options.text: Databases
options.explanation: is correct. Databases are one of the primary object types supported in replication and failover groups, allowing their tables, views, and other objects to be synchronized to secondary accounts.

options.text: Network policies
options.explanation: is correct. Network policies are an account-level object type that can be included in replication groups (on Business Critical Edition and above), ensuring consistent access control across primary and secondary accounts.

options.text: Snowsight worksheet contents
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowsight worksheet contents are UI state stored per user and are not a replicable account object type.

options.text: Stage file compression settings stored in CSV headers
options.explanation: is incorrect. CSV file metadata such as compression hints in headers is not an account object and cannot be part of a replication or failover group.

questions.stem: What does a Snowflake resource monitor primarily track?

options.text: Micro-partition count per table
options.explanation: is incorrect. Micro-partition counts are storage metadata visible in table information views such as INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS. Resource monitors do not interact with storage metadata.

options.text: Number of active users in the account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Active user session counts can be observed through ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS, not resource monitors.

options.text: Virtual warehouse credit consumption
options.explanation: is correct. Resource monitors track the number of Snowflake credits consumed by virtual warehouses (or the account overall) within a specified interval, and can trigger notifications or suspension actions when credit thresholds are reached.

options.text: Stage file sizes uploaded via PUT
options.explanation: is incorrect. Stage file sizes are observable via LIST commands or storage views. Resource monitors are strictly tied to compute credit consumption.

questions.stem: Which actions can a resource monitor take when a credit threshold is reached?

options.text: Rename the warehouse to indicate it is over budget
options.explanation: is incorrect. Resource monitors have no capability to rename a warehouse. Renaming an object requires an ALTER WAREHOUSE statement executed with the appropriate privilege.

options.text: Convert all permanent tables to transient to reduce storage costs
options.explanation: is incorrect. Table type (permanent vs. transient) is set at creation time or changed via ALTER TABLE. Resource monitors are entirely compute-focused and have no storage management actions.

options.text: Grant IMPORTED PRIVILEGES to the SYSADMIN role
options.explanation: is incorrect. IMPORTED PRIVILEGES is a privilege used in data sharing contexts. Resource monitors cannot modify grants.

options.text: Notify account administrators and optionally suspend the warehouse
options.explanation: is correct. A resource monitor can send email notifications to designated account administrators. Depending on the configured action, it can also suspend the warehouse (letting running queries finish) or suspend it immediately (terminating running queries) when a threshold percentage is reached.

questions.stem: When are multi-cluster warehouses most beneficial?

options.text: When a single massive ETL query needs faster execution
options.explanation: is incorrect. A single very large query benefits from a larger warehouse size (more nodes per cluster), not more clusters. Each query executes within a single cluster.

options.text: When Time Travel retention needs to be extended beyond 90 days
options.explanation: is incorrect. Time Travel retention is controlled by the DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS parameter on the object or account, not by warehouse cluster count.

options.text: When data from an external stage needs to be loaded more reliably
options.explanation: is incorrect. Data loading reliability with COPY INTO depends on file format settings, stage configuration, and error handling options — not on multi-cluster warehouse configuration.

options.text: When many concurrent users are queuing because the single-cluster warehouse is saturated
options.explanation: is correct. Multi-cluster warehouses automatically spin up additional clusters when the number of queued queries exceeds the capacity of a single cluster, reducing wait times for concurrent users. This is the canonical use case.

questions.stem: Which warehouse setting most directly prevents credits from being consumed when no queries are running?

options.text: AUTO_SUSPEND
options.explanation: is correct. AUTO_SUSPEND defines the number of seconds a warehouse can be idle before Snowflake automatically suspends it, stopping credit consumption. Setting a low AUTO_SUSPEND value (e.g., 60 seconds) for infrequently used warehouses is a primary cost-control mechanism.

options.text: QUERY_TAG
options.explanation: is incorrect. QUERY_TAG is a session or query metadata label used for attribution and monitoring in query history; it does not affect credit consumption.

options.text: CLUSTER BY
options.explanation: is incorrect. CLUSTER BY defines a clustering key for a table to improve micro-partition pruning. It is a storage optimization setting, not a warehouse compute setting.

options.text: MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL
options.explanation: is incorrect. MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL controls the maximum number of concurrent SQL executions within a single cluster. It does not affect whether a warehouse idles and continues to consume credits.

questions.stem: Queries on a shared warehouse are taking much longer than usual and some are not starting. What should an administrator investigate first?

options.text: Whether the account's network policy has blocked additional IP addresses
options.explanation: is incorrect. A network policy change would prevent connections from affected IPs entirely; users would receive a connection error, not slow queries.

options.text: Whether the SYSADMIN role has been accidentally revoked
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN controls object creation and management. Revoking it would not prevent active sessions from running queries on already-accessible objects.

options.text: Whether the warehouse is queueing queries due to concurrency or sizing constraints
options.explanation: is correct. When queries slow down or queue on a shared warehouse, the most common causes are: (1) too many concurrent queries for the warehouse's cluster size, (2) a warehouse that is undersized for the workloads, or (3) auto-suspension pausing the warehouse between queries. The query profile and warehouse load monitoring views expose queue depth and execution time directly.

options.text: Whether a data sharing provider has revoked IMPORTED PRIVILEGES
options.explanation: is incorrect. IMPORTED PRIVILEGES are used to allow roles in a consumer account to query shared databases. Revoking them would cause query failures on shared objects, but would not explain queueing on a local warehouse.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake tool provides the most detailed breakdown of operator-level execution steps within a single query?

options.text: Network policies configuration page
options.explanation: is incorrect. The network policies page manages IP-based access restrictions; it contains no query execution information.

options.text: Resource monitor threshold history
options.explanation: is incorrect. Resource monitor threshold history shows when credit thresholds were crossed and what actions were taken; it does not show query-level execution details.

options.text: Organization usage views
options.explanation: is incorrect. Organization usage views provide aggregate credit and storage metrics across accounts; they do not provide per-query execution plans.

options.text: Query profile in Snowsight
options.explanation: is correct. The query profile in Snowsight displays the execution plan broken down by operator nodes (TableScan, Filter, Aggregate, etc.) with statistics like row count, bytes processed, and time spent per node, enabling precise performance diagnosis.

questions.stem: Which two parameters directly control how a multi-cluster warehouse scales its number of active clusters? (Select TWO)

options.text: MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT
options.explanation: is correct. MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT sets the minimum number of clusters that must remain running at all times, even when the warehouse is idle — this avoids cold-start latency for always-on workloads.

options.text: MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT
options.explanation: is correct. MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT sets the upper bound on how many clusters can spin up during peak concurrency, capping the maximum credit spend during burst periods.

options.text: DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS
options.explanation: is incorrect. DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS is an object-level parameter that controls how long historical data is retained for Time Travel. It has no effect on multi-cluster scaling.

options.text: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
options.explanation: is incorrect. STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS is a session or object parameter that cancels queries exceeding the specified duration. It is unrelated to cluster scaling.

questions.stem: What is the key operational difference between the Standard and Economy scaling policies for a multi-cluster warehouse?

options.text: Economy policy runs each cluster at a larger warehouse size to minimize cluster count
options.explanation: is incorrect. Scaling policies do not change the warehouse size (number of nodes per cluster). The warehouse SIZE parameter controls that independently.

options.text: Standard policy prevents auto-resume, while Economy policy allows it
options.explanation: is incorrect. Both Standard and Economy policies support auto-resume and auto-suspend. AUTO_RESUME is a separate warehouse parameter that applies regardless of scaling policy.

options.text: Economy policy waits longer before starting additional clusters to save credits; Standard starts additional clusters more aggressively to reduce query wait time
options.explanation: is correct. With the Economy scaling policy, Snowflake waits until it determines that a new cluster would run for at least 6 minutes before starting it, prioritizing credit savings over immediate concurrency. With the Standard policy, Snowflake starts additional clusters as soon as queueing is detected, prioritizing performance over cost.

options.text: Standard policy disables the result cache to ensure all queries run fresh
options.explanation: is incorrect. The result cache is a global Snowflake service layer feature unaffected by warehouse scaling policy configuration.

questions.stem: Which warehouse change most directly gives a single large query access to more compute resources?

options.text: Enabling multi-cluster mode by setting MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT to 4
options.explanation: is incorrect. Additional clusters (multi-cluster mode) improve concurrency by routing different queries to different clusters. A single query still runs on one cluster, so adding more clusters does not speed it up.

options.text: Increasing the warehouse size from Small to Large
options.explanation: is correct. Each warehouse size tier doubles the number of compute nodes in the cluster. Increasing from Small (1 node) to Large (4 nodes) gives a single query more CPU, memory, and I/O bandwidth, which directly reduces execution time for large scans.

options.text: Attaching a resource monitor with a high credit quota
options.explanation: is incorrect. A resource monitor's credit quota is an administrative spending limit — it does not allocate additional compute to a query. Setting a higher quota simply allows more credits to be spent before suspension.

options.text: Creating a secure view over the target table
options.explanation: is incorrect. A secure view hides the underlying query definition from non-owners for data protection purposes. It has no effect on compute resource allocation.

questions.stem: Which statement about Snowflake serverless features is most accurate?

options.text: Serverless features use a customer-managed virtual warehouse and bill through that warehouse's credits
options.explanation: is incorrect. Serverless features explicitly do not use customer-managed virtual warehouses. They consume compute from Snowflake's own managed pool.

options.text: Serverless features are free of charge when all customer-managed warehouses are suspended
options.explanation: is incorrect. Serverless compute is billed based on actual usage regardless of whether customer warehouses are running or suspended. There is no free tier tied to warehouse suspension state.

options.text: Snowflake manages the compute for serverless features, but their usage still accrues Snowflake credits
options.explanation: is correct. Serverless features such as Snowpipe, automatic clustering, materialized view maintenance, and search optimization use Snowflake-managed compute that scales independently of customer warehouses. However, they are not free — they accrue credits that appear in ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY and related views.

options.text: Serverless features cannot be monitored through ACCOUNT_USAGE views
options.explanation: is incorrect. Serverless feature consumption is visible in ACCOUNT_USAGE through views such as SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY, PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY, and AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY.

questions.stem: A data team wants to receive an email warning when 80% of the monthly credit budget is used, but does not want jobs to stop automatically. What should they configure?

options.text: A resource monitor with a SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE action at the 80% threshold
options.explanation: is incorrect. SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE would kill all running queries and suspend the warehouse as soon as the threshold is crossed — the opposite of what is required. This action is appropriate for hard budget stops, not warnings.

options.text: A network policy with an allowed IP list limited to admin IPs at 80% usage
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network policies control which IP addresses can connect to Snowflake. They are unrelated to credit consumption monitoring.

options.text: An account parameter setting CREDIT_QUOTA to 80% of the monthly allocation
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREDIT_QUOTA is a parameter you set on a resource monitor object itself, not a standalone account parameter. And setting it to 80% of the allocation would still require pairing it with a NOTIFY action to avoid auto-suspension.

options.text: A resource monitor with a NOTIFY action at the 80% threshold
options.explanation: is correct. A resource monitor threshold can be set to the NOTIFY action, which sends an email alert to designated account administrators when the specified credit percentage is reached, without taking any action against the warehouse.

questions.stem: A BI tool runs 200 simultaneous dashboard queries from 50 concurrent users. Adding more clusters to a multi-cluster warehouse is the most appropriate tuning approach. Why?

options.text: More clusters increase the amount of local disk cache available per query
options.explanation: is incorrect. Local disk cache (the warehouse cache) is a per-cluster in-memory/SSD cache. Adding more clusters adds more cache instances but does not increase the cache for any individual query's cluster.

options.text: More clusters extend the Time Travel retention window for the BI queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. Time Travel retention is governed by DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS, not by warehouse cluster count.

options.text: More clusters allow concurrent queries to be distributed across separate compute pools, reducing queue depth
options.explanation: is correct. Each cluster in a multi-cluster warehouse is an independent compute pool. When concurrent queries exceed a single cluster's capacity, Snowflake routes overflow queries to additional clusters, reducing queue time for all users.

options.text: More clusters bypass the result cache, ensuring dashboard data is always fresh
options.explanation: is incorrect. Result cache bypass is controlled by the USE_CACHED_RESULT session parameter or by query patterns that modify underlying data. Cluster count has no bearing on result cache usage.

questions.stem: What does setting AUTO_RESUME = TRUE on a virtual warehouse enable?

options.text: The warehouse automatically fails over to a secondary region when unavailable
options.explanation: is incorrect. Failover to a secondary region is handled by failover groups and replication features, not by the AUTO_RESUME warehouse parameter.

options.text: The warehouse automatically restarts from a suspended state when a new query is submitted
options.explanation: is correct. When AUTO_RESUME = TRUE, Snowflake automatically resumes a suspended warehouse when a SQL statement that requires compute is submitted against it, eliminating the need for manual RESUME commands.

options.text: Masking policies on the warehouse's tables are automatically refreshed
options.explanation: is incorrect. Masking policies are security objects applied to columns and are refreshed/evaluated at query time. They have no connection to warehouse AUTO_RESUME behavior.

options.text: The warehouse automatically resizes to a larger size when queries queue
options.explanation: is incorrect. Automatic resizing is not a native Snowflake warehouse feature. Scaling out (more clusters) is possible via multi-cluster configuration, but changing warehouse SIZE requires manual ALTER WAREHOUSE commands or an external automation.

questions.stem: Which statement about Snowflake role hierarchy is correct?

options.text: Granting role R1 to role R2 means R2 inherits all privileges held by R1
options.explanation: is correct. In Snowflake's RBAC model, when role R1 is granted to role R2, R2 inherits all privileges that R1 holds. This allows hierarchical privilege accumulation so that higher-level roles automatically gain all the access of lower-level roles.

options.text: Each user may only have one role active at a time and cannot change it
options.explanation: is incorrect. A user can switch their active primary role at any time using USE ROLE, and can also use secondary roles within a session to broaden available privileges.

options.text: System-defined roles cannot own data objects
options.explanation: is incorrect. System-defined roles like SYSADMIN can and do own objects. In fact, best practice is to have SYSADMIN own all production data objects.

options.text: Privileges flow from parent roles down to child roles only
options.explanation: is incorrect. Privileges flow upward — a child role's privileges are inherited by the parent role that the child is granted to, not the reverse.

questions.stem: An analyst role needs to be able to use a specific warehouse to run queries. Which privilege must be granted on that warehouse?

options.text: OWNERSHIP
options.explanation: is incorrect. OWNERSHIP confers full control over the warehouse object including the ability to alter or drop it — far more than an analyst should have for routine querying.

options.text: OPERATE
options.explanation: is incorrect. OPERATE allows a role to start, stop, resize, and abort queries on the warehouse, but does not by itself grant the ability to run queries through it. USAGE is the correct privilege for query execution.

options.text: USAGE
options.explanation: is correct. USAGE on a warehouse allows a role to use that warehouse to execute queries and DML statements. It is the minimum privilege required to run workloads on a warehouse.

options.text: MONITOR
options.explanation: is incorrect. MONITOR allows a role to view the warehouse's credit usage and query history, but does not grant the ability to run queries on it.

questions.stem: What do future grants help administrators accomplish in Snowflake?

options.text: Automatically encrypt all future tables created in a schema
options.explanation: is incorrect. Encryption in Snowflake is automatic for all data at rest. It is not controlled through privilege grants.

options.text: Pre-size future warehouses created by SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse sizing is set at creation time via CREATE WAREHOUSE SIZE = ... or altered with ALTER WAREHOUSE. Future grants apply to object privileges, not warehouse configuration parameters.

options.text: Automatically share future databases with all consumers in an organization
options.explanation: is incorrect. Data sharing to consumers requires explicit GRANT ON DATABASE ... TO SHARE and then GRANT ON SHARE commands. Future grants do not control share membership.

options.text: Automatically apply specified privileges to new objects created in a database or schema after the grant is defined
options.explanation: is correct. Future grants (GRANT ... ON FUTURE ...) allow administrators to specify that any object of a given type created in a schema or database will automatically receive the defined privilege for a role, eliminating the need to manually grant access each time a developer creates a new table or view.

questions.stem: Which two privileges are required for a role to successfully run a SELECT query on a table? (Select TWO)

options.text: USAGE on the database and schema containing the table
options.explanation: is correct. USAGE must be granted on both the database and the schema that contain the target table. Without USAGE on the parent containers, the role cannot navigate to the object even if it has SELECT on the table itself.

options.text: SELECT on the table or view
options.explanation: is correct. SELECT on the table (or view) is required to read its rows. Without SELECT, the query will fail with an insufficient privileges error even if the role has USAGE on the containers.

options.text: OWNERSHIP on the warehouse being used
options.explanation: is incorrect. OWNERSHIP on the warehouse is not needed to run queries through it — USAGE on the warehouse is the correct privilege. OWNERSHIP grants full DDL control over the warehouse.

options.text: CREATE USER privilege on the account
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE USER is a system privilege for creating new users. It is entirely unrelated to querying data.

questions.stem: Why is the OWNERSHIP privilege treated differently from other privileges in Snowflake?

options.text: OWNERSHIP can be held by multiple roles simultaneously for joint control
options.explanation: is incorrect. Only one role can hold OWNERSHIP of an object at a time. It is not a shared privilege.

options.text: OWNERSHIP is transferred from one role to another rather than duplicated, and confers full DDL control over the object
options.explanation: is correct. Exactly one role owns any given object at a time. OWNERSHIP is transferred via GRANT OWNERSHIP … REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS, moving complete DDL authority from the current owner to the new owner. This differs from other privileges like SELECT that can be granted to many roles simultaneously.

options.text: OWNERSHIP is available only on tables, not on other object types
options.explanation: is incorrect. OWNERSHIP applies to all Snowflake object types — warehouses, databases, schemas, tables, views, stages, tasks, pipes, and so on.

options.text: OWNERSHIP disables Time Travel on the owned object to reduce storage costs
options.explanation: is incorrect. Time Travel retention is controlled by DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS. Transferring OWNERSHIP has no effect on Time Travel configuration or storage behavior.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake object type is applied to a table column to control how sensitive values are displayed based on the querying role?

options.text: Row access policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. A row access policy controls which rows a role can see in a query result — it filters at the row level, not the column level.

options.text: Resource monitor
options.explanation: is incorrect. A resource monitor tracks credit consumption on virtual warehouses. It has no interaction with data column values.

options.text: Masking policy
options.explanation: is correct. A masking policy is a schema-level object that defines a SQL expression to transform column values at query time. When applied to a column, it can return the real value, a masked value, or NULL depending on the querying role or other conditions.

options.text: Network policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. A network policy restricts which client IP addresses can connect to Snowflake. It operates at the connection level, not the data level.

questions.stem: A stored procedure must access objects that the calling user does not have direct access to, using only the procedure owner's grants. Which execution rights model achieves this?

options.text: Caller's rights procedure
options.explanation: is incorrect. A caller's rights procedure executes with the privileges of the calling user's current role. If the caller lacks access to an object, the procedure will also fail to access it.

options.text: Reader account procedure
options.explanation: is incorrect. A reader account is a type of Snowflake account provided to data-sharing consumers who do not have their own Snowflake accounts. It is not a stored procedure concept.

options.text: Owner's rights procedure
options.explanation: is correct. An owner's rights stored procedure runs with the privileges of the role that owns the procedure, not the role of the calling user. This allows a controlled escalation of privilege: a user can execute the procedure and indirectly access objects they cannot reach directly.

options.text: Secondary role procedure
options.explanation: is incorrect. Secondary roles broaden the session's privilege set using additional roles already granted to the user. They do not allow a procedure to use privileges the calling user doesn't hold.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of creating a custom functional role such as ANALYST_READ_ONLY?

options.text: To group the specific privileges needed for a job function and assign them to users with that function
options.explanation: is correct. Custom functional roles model real-world job responsibilities by bundling the exact privileges needed (e.g., SELECT on specific schemas, USAGE on a warehouse) into a named role. Users with that job function are granted the role, enabling least-privilege access that scales easily as teams grow.

options.text: To store staged files associated with analyst work
options.explanation: is incorrect. Stages are storage objects; storing files in a stage is unrelated to role design.

options.text: To replace the INFORMATION_SCHEMA schema for that role
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA is a built-in schema present in every database that exposes metadata views. It cannot be replaced by a role.

options.text: To automatically resize the warehouse when the analyst runs a query
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse resizing is a DDL operation requiring OPERATE or OWNERSHIP on the warehouse. Functional roles do not have auto-resize behavior.

questions.stem: Which statement about granting privileges directly to individual users rather than to roles is most accurate?

options.text: It is the recommended approach in all production Snowflake environments
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake's access control best practices explicitly recommend role-based access control, not direct user grants, for all production environments.

options.text: It is required to grant IMPORTED PRIVILEGES for data sharing
options.explanation: is incorrect. IMPORTED PRIVILEGES is a privilege granted to a role on a shared database to allow roles in the consumer account to query the share. It is not granted to individual users.

options.text: It works but does not scale well, and role-based grants are easier to audit and revoke
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake does support granting some privileges to users directly, but role-based grants are strongly preferred. Roles group related grants, can be revoked in one step (revoke the role), and their assignments are easily audited in GRANTS_TO_ROLES and GRANTS_TO_USERS views.

options.text: It eliminates the need for MFA because individual grants are more secure
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA is an authentication-layer control independent of how privileges are structured. Direct user grants do not weaken or strengthen MFA requirements.

questions.stem: Beyond SELECT on a secure view, what container privilege is also required for a role to query it?

options.text: OPERATE on the virtual warehouse being used
options.explanation: is incorrect. OPERATE on a warehouse allows management operations (suspend, resize). The privilege needed to run queries through a warehouse is USAGE, not OPERATE.

options.text: USAGE on the database and schema that contain the secure view
options.explanation: is correct. Regardless of whether a view is standard or secure, the querying role must have USAGE on every container in the hierarchy — the database and the schema — before it can resolve and query the view. This is a fundamental requirement in Snowflake's privilege model.

options.text: PUBLIC role membership to satisfy the secure view trust model
options.explanation: is incorrect. PUBLIC is a default role granted to everyone, but membership in PUBLIC does not satisfy any special requirement for secure views. Secure views simply prevent non-owners from seeing the view definition.

options.text: OWNERSHIP on the underlying base tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. OWNERSHIP on base tables would give full DDL control — far more than needed, and not required. The base table privilege requirements for a secure view's querying role are handled through the view owner's rights (for owner's-rights views) or the caller's grants.

questions.stem: Which SQL command activates secondary roles for privilege evaluation in the current session?

options.text: ALTER SESSION SET USE_SECONDARY_ROLES = ALL
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER SESSION can set parameters like QUERY_TAG or TIMEZONE, but USE_SECONDARY_ROLES is not a session parameter set with ALTER SESSION — it is controlled with the USE SECONDARY ROLES command.

options.text: OPEN FUTURE GRANTS ALL
options.explanation: is incorrect. OPEN FUTURE GRANTS is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

options.text: GRANT ALL ROLES TO SESSION
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT ALL ROLES TO SESSION is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

options.text: USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL
options.explanation: is correct. USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL activates all roles granted to the current user as secondary roles in the session, expanding the set of privileges available for SQL evaluation without changing the primary role.

questions.stem: What is a primary advantage of using database roles compared to account roles for managing access within a shared database?

options.text: Database roles can be granted to warehouses to give them compute access
options.explanation: is incorrect. Roles — database or account — are granted to users and other roles, not to warehouses. Warehouses are compute resources that roles use; they do not receive privilege grants.

options.text: Database roles are scoped to a single database and can be included directly in a data share, making privilege management modular for consumers
options.explanation: is correct. Database roles are scoped to a specific database, so their privileges cannot accidentally leak to objects outside it. Critically, database roles can be included in a Snowflake data share, allowing data providers to define and ship access tiers (e.g., READ_ONLY, ANALYST) directly to consumers without exposing account-level roles.

options.text: Database roles replace network policies within the database boundary
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network policies operate at the connection level (IP allowlisting) and are entirely separate from database-level privilege constructs.

options.text: Database roles allow the database to publish listings on Snowflake Marketplace
options.explanation: is incorrect. Marketplace listings are published by providers through the Snowflake Marketplace UI or API, not by granting database roles.

questions.stem: A team needs different users to see only rows relevant to their sales region without duplicating tables. Which Snowflake feature is most appropriate?

options.text: Row access policy attached to the table
options.explanation: is correct. A row access policy is a schema-level object attached to a table or view that uses a SQL condition to filter which rows are returned for a given user or role. The same underlying table is used for all users, with each user's query automatically filtered to only the rows matching their region mapping.

options.text: Masking policy applied to the region column
options.explanation: is incorrect. A masking policy operates on column values — it returns a masked or unmasked value for a column — but it does not filter which rows appear in the result set.

options.text: Resource monitor with a per-region credit limit
options.explanation: is incorrect. A resource monitor controls credit consumption on warehouses. It has no capability to partition data visibility by region.

options.text: Separate temporary stages per region
options.explanation: is incorrect. Separate stages per region are external storage locations for loading or unloading data. They do not control row-level visibility in query results.

questions.stem: What does a Snowflake network policy control?

options.text: Which IP addresses or ranges are allowed or blocked from connecting to Snowflake
options.explanation: is correct. A network policy defines allowed IP address ranges (and optionally blocked ranges) for connecting to the Snowflake service. When active at the account level it applies to all users; when applied to a specific user it overrides the account-level policy for that user.

options.text: Which virtual warehouse size is permitted for each role
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse size permissions are governed by the OPERATE and MODIFY privileges on the warehouse object, not by network policies.

options.text: Which columns in a table are masked at query time
options.explanation: is incorrect. Column masking is controlled by masking policies applied to table columns. Network policies have no awareness of data content.

options.text: How object ownership is transferred between roles
options.explanation: is incorrect. Object ownership transfer is performed using GRANT OWNERSHIP ... REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS. It is a privilege management operation unrelated to network access control.

questions.stem: Which authentication method is most appropriate for an automated ETL service that runs without user interaction?

options.text: Username and password stored in the ETL orchestrator's secret manager
options.explanation: is incorrect. While password-based credentials in a secret manager can work, password authentication is weaker than key-pair auth for automated services because passwords can expire, be reused, or be leaked through logs.

options.text: Key-pair authentication using an RSA private key
options.explanation: is correct. Key-pair authentication uses an RSA private key (stored securely by the ETL tool) paired with a public key registered on the Snowflake user. It avoids embedding a plaintext password and is designed for non-interactive service connections.

options.text: MFA with a push notification to the service account's phone
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA with push notifications requires a human to approve each login on a mobile device. This is incompatible with fully automated, unattended service connections.

options.text: Browser-based SSO via an identity provider
options.explanation: is incorrect. Browser-based SSO requires an interactive browser redirect to an identity provider. It cannot be used in headless automation contexts.

questions.stem: What is the primary operational benefit of integrating Snowflake with an enterprise identity provider using federated authentication?

options.text: Snowflake automatically resizes warehouses when the IdP authenticates a new user
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse resizing is a DDL operation unrelated to authentication configuration.

options.text: Micro-partitions are consolidated during IdP authentication to improve performance
options.explanation: is incorrect. Micro-partition consolidation (reclustering) is driven by automatic clustering services or manual ALTER TABLE ... RECLUSTER. Authentication events have no effect on storage layout.

options.text: Users authenticate through the enterprise IdP using their corporate credentials, enabling centralized access management and SSO
options.explanation: is correct. Federated authentication (SSO) delegates the login flow to the enterprise identity provider (e.g., Okta, Entra ID). Users log in once with their corporate credentials, and the IdP issues a SAML assertion to Snowflake. This centralizes user lifecycle management — provisioning, deprovisioning, and MFA — in the IdP rather than requiring separate Snowflake passwords.

options.text: Data shares no longer require explicit IMPORTED PRIVILEGES grants after IdP setup
options.explanation: is incorrect. IMPORTED PRIVILEGES must still be explicitly granted to allow consumer roles to query shared databases. SSO configuration does not alter privilege requirements.

questions.stem: Which statement about multi-factor authentication (MFA) in Snowflake is accurate?

options.text: MFA replaces role-based access control for accounts in Business Critical edition
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA is an authentication mechanism that complements access control — it does not replace RBAC. Role hierarchies and privilege grants operate independently of MFA.

options.text: MFA requires a second verification factor at login time, and can be enforced via authentication policies
options.explanation: is correct. MFA in Snowflake requires users to provide a second factor (typically a TOTP code or push notification through Duo) in addition to their password. Administrators can enforce MFA enrollment using authentication policies attached at the user or account level.

options.text: MFA is only available for service accounts using key-pair authentication
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA is designed for interactive human logins, not for service accounts. Service accounts typically use key-pair authentication instead, which provides strong non-interactive authentication without requiring a second factor.

options.text: MFA is configured per warehouse, not per user
options.explanation: is incorrect. MFA is configured at the user or account level through authentication policies, not per warehouse. Warehouses are compute resources with no concept of authentication factors.

questions.stem: Which two controls specifically reduce the risk of unauthorized login to a Snowflake account? (Select TWO)

options.text: Network policies restricting allowed source IP ranges
options.explanation: is correct. Network policies restrict which source IP addresses can establish a connection to Snowflake, blocking login attempts from unauthorized locations before credentials are even evaluated.

options.text: Multi-factor authentication for interactive users
options.explanation: is correct. MFA requires a second factor beyond a password, making it much harder for an attacker who has stolen credentials to complete a login.

options.text: Setting AUTO_SUSPEND to a low value on warehouses
options.explanation: is incorrect. AUTO_SUSPEND controls how long a warehouse stays running while idle before shutting down compute. It reduces credit waste but has no effect on authentication security.

options.text: Defining clustering keys on large tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Clustering keys improve micro-partition pruning for large table scans. They are a query performance feature unrelated to authentication or access control.

questions.stem: Why must administrators be cautious when granting privileges to the PUBLIC role?

options.text: PUBLIC is automatically granted to every user in the account, so any privilege on PUBLIC is effectively granted to all users
options.explanation: is correct. The PUBLIC role is automatically granted to every user in a Snowflake account and cannot be revoked. Any privilege granted to PUBLIC is therefore implicitly available to every user, including newly created users and service accounts — making it a very broad grant that is easy to overlook during access reviews.

options.text: PUBLIC can only be granted warehouse OWNERSHIP, which makes it dangerous
options.explanation: is incorrect. PUBLIC can receive many types of privilege grants, not only warehouse OWNERSHIP. In fact, granting OWNERSHIP to PUBLIC would be extremely dangerous for exactly the reason in option A, but this is a description of a risk, not a limitation of PUBLIC.

options.text: PUBLIC automatically bypasses masking policies on columns
options.explanation: is incorrect. PUBLIC does not automatically bypass masking policies. Masking policies evaluate the querying role against the policy's conditions regardless of whether PUBLIC is involved.

options.text: PUBLIC is granted the ACCOUNTADMIN role by default in new accounts
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN is never granted to PUBLIC by default. ACCOUNTADMIN is a high-privilege role that must be explicitly granted only to designated administrator users.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature allows a customer to participate in encryption using their own key managed in an external key management service?

options.text: Zero-copy cloning
options.explanation: is incorrect. Zero-copy cloning creates a metadata-only copy of a table or database that shares underlying storage with the source. It is a storage efficiency feature unrelated to encryption key management.

options.text: Tri-Secret Secure
options.explanation: is correct. Tri-Secret Secure is a Business Critical edition feature that combines Snowflake's own encryption key with a customer-managed key (CMK) from an external KMS (e.g., AWS KMS). Both keys are required to decrypt data, so Snowflake alone cannot access a customer's data without the customer's key.

options.text: Reader accounts
options.explanation: is incorrect. Reader accounts are lightweight Snowflake accounts provisioned by a data provider to allow external consumers to query shared data without their own Snowflake account. They have no encryption key management role.

options.text: Result cache
options.explanation: is incorrect. Result cache stores the output of previously executed queries for reuse. It is a performance optimization with no connection to encryption key management.

questions.stem: What does TLS protect in Snowflake?

options.text: Network traffic between clients and the Snowflake service
options.explanation: is correct. TLS (Transport Layer Security) encrypts data in transit — specifically the network traffic flowing between Snowflake clients (drivers, connectors, Snowsight) and the Snowflake service endpoint. All Snowflake connections enforce TLS.

options.text: Data stored in micro-partitions on cloud storage
options.explanation: is incorrect. Data stored in micro-partitions on cloud storage is protected by Snowflake's at-rest encryption using AES-256, not TLS. TLS only applies while data is moving over a network connection.

options.text: Marketplace listing metadata
options.explanation: is incorrect. Marketplace listing metadata is managed through the Snowflake platform and protected as part of Snowflake's overall security model. TLS protects the transport of that data over the network, but "marketplace listing metadata" is not what TLS specifically targets.

options.text: Query result cache entries on the warehouse
options.explanation: is incorrect. Query result cache entries live in Snowflake's service layer, not on the warehouse (virtual compute). TLS does not specifically protect cache entries — those are secured by Snowflake's internal data protection mechanisms.

questions.stem: A company wants to restrict a third-party contractor's Snowflake access to connections originating from the contractor's known office IP range only. Which feature should be used?

options.text: A masking policy applied to all tables the contractor queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. A masking policy protects column values at query time by returning masked output to unauthorized roles. It does not restrict which network locations a user can connect from.

options.text: A network policy applied to the contractor's Snowflake user
options.explanation: is correct. A network policy applied to a specific user restricts that user's connections to the allowed IP ranges defined in the policy. When the contractor attempts to connect from an IP outside the allowed list, the connection is rejected before authentication proceeds.

options.text: A resource monitor assigned to the warehouse used by the contractor
options.explanation: is incorrect. A resource monitor tracks credit consumption and can suspend a warehouse when a budget threshold is reached. It has no awareness of the source IP of connecting clients.

options.text: A row access policy filtering rows by contractor role
options.explanation: is incorrect. A row access policy filters which rows a user or role can see in a query result. It is a data-visibility control, not a network connection control.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of defining a clustering key on a large Snowflake table?

options.text: To grant SELECT access to roles based on the clustered column
options.explanation: is incorrect. Column-level access is controlled by masking policies and column-level grants, not clustering keys. A clustering key affects physical storage organization, not access control.

options.text: To improve micro-partition pruning for queries that filter on the clustering column
options.explanation: is correct. A clustering key co-locates rows with similar values for the specified column(s) within micro-partitions. This allows Snowflake's query optimizer to skip (prune) micro-partitions whose range metadata does not overlap the filter predicate, reducing the amount of data scanned.

options.text: To enable replication of the table to a secondary account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Table replication is controlled by including the database in a replication or failover group. Defining a clustering key on a table has no bearing on replication eligibility.

options.text: To reduce the Time Travel retention period for the table
options.explanation: is incorrect. Time Travel retention is governed by DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS at the object or account level. Clustering keys do not modify retention settings.

questions.stem: Under what condition can the Snowflake result cache serve a query without executing it against the warehouse?

options.text: When the querying role holds OWNERSHIP on the target table
options.explanation: is incorrect. The result cache is available regardless of the querying role's privilege level, as long as the role has the necessary SELECT privileges. OWNERSHIP is not required.

options.text: When the warehouse was manually resumed rather than auto-resumed
options.explanation: is incorrect. How the warehouse was resumed (auto or manual) has no effect on whether the result cache is checked. The result cache operates at the cloud services layer, above the warehouse.

options.text: When the query runs during off-peak hours as defined by the resource monitor schedule
options.explanation: is incorrect. The result cache is not schedule-aware. Resource monitors do not define off-peak windows that affect caching behavior.

options.text: When the query text is identical and the underlying data has not changed within the cache validity window
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake's result cache stores query results for up to 24 hours. A subsequent query can reuse the cached result if: (1) the query text matches exactly, (2) the underlying table data has not changed since the result was cached, and (3) the querying user has access to the referenced objects. No warehouse compute is needed.

questions.stem: What does Snowflake Time Travel primarily allow?

options.text: Automatic failover of the account to a secondary region
options.explanation: is incorrect. Regional failover is handled by failover groups and replication, not by Time Travel. Time Travel is a data recovery mechanism within a single account.

options.text: Automatic cleanup of expired role grants
options.explanation: is incorrect. Expired grants are managed through Snowflake's grant lifecycle and are not cleaned up by Time Travel. Role grants do not have expiry dates by default.

options.text: Querying and restoring historical versions of data within the configured retention period
options.explanation: is correct. Time Travel enables querying data as it existed at a specific point in the past using AT or BEFORE clauses, and restoring dropped or modified tables/schemas/databases using UNDROP or CLONE AT. The retention window is 1 day by default (up to 90 days on Enterprise edition).

options.text: Free unlimited historical storage for all table types
options.explanation: is incorrect. Time Travel storage is billed at standard Snowflake storage rates. It is not free, and the retention period is bounded by DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS (maximum 90 days for permanent tables on Enterprise edition and above).

questions.stem: Which warehouse action is the most direct way to reduce execution time for a single large, complex query?

options.text: Enable multi-cluster mode by increasing MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT
options.explanation: is incorrect. Additional clusters serve additional concurrent queries — each query still executes within a single cluster. Adding clusters does not speed up a single query.

options.text: Increase the warehouse size (e.g., from Medium to Large)
options.explanation: is correct. Each warehouse size tier doubles the number of compute nodes in the cluster, giving individual queries more CPU, memory, and I/O bandwidth. A larger warehouse directly reduces execution time for CPU- and memory-intensive single queries.

options.text: Apply a network policy to reduce competing connections
options.explanation: is incorrect. A network policy restricts which source IPs can connect to Snowflake. It does not allocate more compute to queries or reduce resource contention within the warehouse.

options.text: Attach a resource monitor with a higher credit quota
options.explanation: is incorrect. A resource monitor's credit quota is an administrative budget limit. Raising the quota allows more credits to be spent before suspension, but does not give the query additional compute resources.

questions.stem: Why does zero-copy cloning not immediately increase storage costs after creation?

options.text: The clone is stored in a cheaper storage tier that is billed at a discounted rate
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not have tiered storage tiers with different billing rates for clones. All active data (including clone-exclusive micro-partitions) is billed at the same standard rate.

options.text: The clone shares the same underlying micro-partition files as the source and only stores changed data separately after divergence
options.explanation: is correct. Zero-copy cloning creates a new metadata pointer to the same set of micro-partition files as the source. No data is physically copied at creation time. Storage costs only increase as the clone and source diverge through DML operations — each modification creates new micro-partitions exclusive to the modified object.

options.text: The clone disables Time Travel on the source table to reclaim storage
options.explanation: is incorrect. Creating a clone does not alter the source table's Time Travel configuration. Both the source and the clone independently maintain their own Time Travel history after divergence.

options.text: The clone exports data to an external stage and is billed as external storage
options.explanation: is incorrect. Zero-copy clones are internal Snowflake objects stored within Snowflake's managed cloud storage — not external stages. Billing applies once exclusive micro-partitions are created.

questions.stem: Which two features contribute to storage costs beyond the space used by active table data? (Select TWO)

options.text: Time Travel retention
options.explanation: is correct. Time Travel retains historical micro-partition versions for the duration of DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS. These historical versions are billed as additional storage on top of the current active data.

options.text: Fail-safe
options.explanation: is correct. Fail-safe is a non-configurable 7-day recovery window maintained by Snowflake after the Time Travel window expires. Storage consumed during Fail-safe is also billed, though it cannot be queried or restored by the customer directly.

options.text: USE ROLE commands in a session
options.explanation: is incorrect. USE ROLE is a SQL command that switches the active role in a session. It has no storage footprint.

options.text: Snowsight worksheet contents
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowsight worksheet contents (SQL query text saved in the UI) are minimal metadata stored by the Snowflake service. They are not billed as data storage for the customer's account in the same way table data is.

questions.stem: What is the most accurate description of Snowflake's Search Optimization Service?

options.text: A warehouse configuration that enables faster warehouse resume
options.explanation: is incorrect. Search Optimization is a table-level feature, not a warehouse configuration. Warehouse resume latency is affected by warehouse size and auto-resume settings.

options.text: A replacement for role-based access control that restricts who can search for data
options.explanation: is incorrect. Data access control is managed through roles, privileges, masking policies, and row access policies. Search Optimization Service has no role in access control.

options.text: A Marketplace feature that indexes listing metadata for faster product discovery
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake Marketplace uses its own indexing and search mechanisms for listing discovery. Search Optimization Service is a customer-configurable feature for querying their own data tables.

options.text: An optional table-level service that accelerates highly selective point lookup and substring search queries by building auxiliary search access paths
options.explanation: is correct. Search Optimization Service builds and maintains per-table search access paths (persisted structures) that allow the query optimizer to skip large amounts of data for highly selective equality, IN, substring (LIKE), and geospatial queries, without requiring a warehouse to scan all micro-partitions.

questions.stem: When would an administrator most likely create a transient table rather than a permanent table?

options.text: When the table must be replicated across regions as part of a failover group
options.explanation: is incorrect. Both permanent and transient tables can be included in a database that is part of a replication group. The table type does not determine replication eligibility.

options.text: When long-term recovery via Fail-safe is required for compliance
options.explanation: is incorrect. When Fail-safe protection is required for compliance, a permanent table must be used. Transient tables explicitly exclude Fail-safe.

options.text: When the table holds intermediate ETL results that do not need Fail-safe protection and shorter retention is acceptable
options.explanation: is correct. Transient tables have a maximum Time Travel retention of 1 day and no Fail-safe period, which reduces storage overhead for staging and intermediate ETL data that is transient by nature and does not need the full 7-day Fail-safe recovery option.

options.text: When the table must enforce strict column masking across all consumer roles
options.explanation: is incorrect. Masking policies can be applied to columns on any table type — permanent, transient, or temporary. The table type does not affect masking policy applicability.

questions.stem: A query unexpectedly scans far more micro-partitions than anticipated. Which concept should be investigated first?

options.text: Whether the ACCOUNTADMIN role is being used for the query session
options.explanation: is incorrect. The role used for the session affects which objects can be accessed but has no bearing on how many micro-partitions are physically scanned by the storage layer.

options.text: Pruning effectiveness — whether the table's data distribution aligns with the query filter
options.explanation: is correct. Micro-partition pruning allows Snowflake to skip partitions whose column range metadata does not overlap the filter predicate. If the data in the table is not well-ordered for the filter column(s), pruning is ineffective and many more micro-partitions are scanned than necessary. This is the first thing to check in the query profile's TableScan node.

options.text: Whether a reader account quota has been exceeded
options.explanation: is incorrect. Reader account quotas control how many credits a reader account can consume. They do not affect how the storage layer scans micro-partitions for a query running in the provider account.

options.text: Whether the network policy allows the querying client IP
options.explanation: is incorrect. If a network policy blocked the client IP, the connection would be rejected entirely — the query would not run at all, let alone scan micro-partitions.

questions.stem: An auditor wants to see which users ran queries against a sensitive table in the past 30 days and when those queries ran. Which Snowflake capability is most relevant?

options.text: Reader account manager reports
options.explanation: is incorrect. Reader account manager reports pertain to usage by external consumers of shared data through reader accounts. They do not track queries by internal users against internal tables.

options.text: Resource monitor threshold history
options.explanation: is incorrect. Resource monitor threshold history shows when credit thresholds were crossed and what suspension actions were triggered. It contains no information about which users ran which queries.

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY and ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY views
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY records every query executed in the account including user, role, warehouse, start time, and SQL text. ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY tracks which data objects were read or written by each query, enabling precise audit trails for sensitive tables.

options.text: Warehouse size change history in Snowsight
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse size change history shows administrative ALTER WAREHOUSE resize operations. It does not record the queries executed on the warehouse.

questions.stem: Why is the ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY view particularly valuable for compliance requirements such as GDPR or CCPA?

options.text: It automatically masks PII columns in query results for non-admin roles
options.explanation: is incorrect. Column masking is performed by masking policies applied to table columns. ACCESS_HISTORY is a read-only audit view and does not transform query results.

options.text: It resizes warehouses to reduce cost when sensitive data is being queried
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse resizing is a compute management operation unrelated to compliance views or data access patterns.

options.text: It removes old stage files automatically to comply with data retention limits
options.explanation: is incorrect. Stage file lifecycle management is a separate operational concern handled by the customer using REMOVE commands or lifecycle policies on the underlying cloud storage. ACCESS_HISTORY does not manage file deletion.

options.text: It records which data objects were read or written per query, supporting data lineage and access audit trails
options.explanation: is correct. ACCESS_HISTORY tracks the objects (databases, schemas, tables, columns) that were accessed — read or written — for each query. This allows compliance teams to demonstrate to regulators that sensitive data was only accessed by authorized users, and to trace exactly which data objects were involved in each operation.

questions.stem: Which practice best supports a least-privilege compliance posture in Snowflake?

options.text: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to all data engineers to eliminate access request friction
options.explanation: is incorrect. Granting ACCOUNTADMIN to all engineers is the opposite of least privilege — ACCOUNTADMIN grants unrestricted account-level control including billing, security settings, and the ability to drop any object.

options.text: Create narrowly scoped custom roles per job function and periodically review grant assignments
options.explanation: is correct. Least privilege means each user holds only the permissions needed for their current job function, and those permissions are reviewed and trimmed periodically. Custom roles that map exactly to job responsibilities, combined with regular access reviews, are the foundation of a defensible least-privilege posture.

options.text: Store all files in user stages indefinitely to preserve audit evidence
options.explanation: is incorrect. Storing files in user stages indefinitely creates a data retention and privacy risk, not a compliance benefit. Audit evidence for Snowflake queries should be captured through QUERY_HISTORY and ACCESS_HISTORY views, not through indefinite file retention.

options.text: Disable MFA for service desk accounts to reduce password reset workload
options.explanation: is incorrect. Disabling MFA for service desk accounts weakens the overall security posture. Service desk accounts that have elevated access to reset passwords are high-value targets and should have stronger authentication, not weaker.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to enable cross-cloud data sharing between two accounts in the same organization without replicating the data. Which feature makes this possible?

options.text: Zero-copy cloning across accounts
options.explanation: is incorrect. Zero-copy cloning creates a metadata-level copy of an object within the same account (or into the same account via replication). It does not enable cross-account live data access without physical data movement.

options.text: Snowflake data sharing via a share object
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake's data sharing allows a provider account to grant read access to databases, schemas, and tables to one or more consumer accounts through a share object. The consumer queries the data live from the provider's storage — no copy is made and no ETL pipeline is required.

options.text: External tables pointing to the same cloud storage bucket
options.explanation: is incorrect. External tables pointing to the same cloud storage bucket is a possible workaround but requires the consumer to have direct access to the underlying cloud storage, introduces IAM complexity, and does not use Snowflake's governed sharing model.

options.text: A replication group containing only virtual warehouses
options.explanation: is incorrect. Replication groups replicate account objects (databases, users, roles, etc.) to a secondary account — they copy data. Virtual warehouses cannot be included in a replication group.

questions.stem: A warehouse resource monitor is set to SUSPEND at 100% of a 1000-credit monthly quota. A long-running query is in progress when the threshold is crossed. What happens?

options.text: The running query is immediately killed and the warehouse is suspended
options.explanation: is incorrect. Immediately killing running queries is the behavior of the SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE action, not SUSPEND. These are distinct resource monitor actions.

options.text: The running query is allowed to complete, but no new queries start after it finishes, and the warehouse suspends
options.explanation: is correct. The SUSPEND action on a resource monitor allows currently running queries to complete before suspending the warehouse. New queries submitted after the threshold is reached will queue or fail, but any query already executing at the moment of threshold breach is allowed to finish.

options.text: The warehouse is suspended and the query is queued until the next billing cycle
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not queue queries across billing cycle resets. When a warehouse is suspended by a resource monitor, queued queries must wait for the monitor to be cleared or reset before the warehouse can be manually resumed.

options.text: An email notification is sent but no action is taken on the warehouse
options.explanation: is incorrect. NOTIFY is a separate action that sends an alert without affecting the warehouse. The SUSPEND action always suspends the warehouse (after running queries complete) — it does not only notify.

questions.stem: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view is most useful for understanding serverless feature credit consumption such as Snowpipe and automatic clustering?

options.text: WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY tracks credit consumption by customer-managed virtual warehouses only. Serverless features bypass these warehouses and are not captured in this view.

options.text: SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY and PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY
options.explanation: is correct. Serverless features have dedicated ACCOUNT_USAGE views. SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY tracks credits used by serverless tasks, PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY covers Snowpipe ingestion credits, and AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY covers auto-clustering credits — none of which appear in WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY because they do not use customer-managed warehouses.

options.text: NETWORK_POLICY_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. NETWORK_POLICY_HISTORY records changes to network policies (create, alter, drop). It contains no credit consumption data.

options.text: ACCESS_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCESS_HISTORY records which data objects were read or written per query for audit purposes. It does not contain credit billing information.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to transfer account ownership of a Snowflake account to a different organization. Which role is required to initiate this transfer at the organization level?

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN has broad object management privileges within a single account (warehouses, databases, schemas) but has no organization-level authority.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SECURITYADMIN manages security objects, grants, and network policies within an account. It cannot perform cross-account or organization-level administrative actions.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. USERADMIN creates and manages users and roles within an account. It has no organization-level capabilities.

options.text: ORGADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. ORGADMIN is the role with organization-level authority. Only ORGADMIN can perform organization-scoped administrative actions including account lifecycle management such as creating, modifying, or transferring accounts within or between organizations.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake command displays the current active role in a session?

options.text: SHOW ROLE
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW ROLES lists all roles defined in the account; it does not show which role is currently active in the session.

options.text: SELECT CURRENT_ROLE()
options.explanation: is correct. The CURRENT_ROLE() context function returns the name of the primary role active in the current session. It can be used in any SQL context, including SELECT statements, WHERE clauses, and policy condition expressions.

options.text: DESCRIBE SESSION ROLE
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE SESSION ROLE is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

options.text: GET ROLE
options.explanation: is incorrect. GET ROLE is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake edition is required to replicate security objects such as users, roles, and network policies as part of a failover group?

options.text: Standard edition
options.explanation: is incorrect. Standard edition supports limited replication features. Security object replication via failover groups is not available at this tier.

options.text: Enterprise edition
options.explanation: is incorrect. Enterprise edition adds features like multi-cluster warehouses and extended Time Travel, but full account-level security object replication in failover groups requires Business Critical edition.

options.text: Business Critical edition
options.explanation: is correct. Replication of account-level security objects — users, roles, warehouses, resource monitors, network policies, and authentication policies — requires Business Critical edition (or higher). Database replication (tables and views) is available on all editions, but the broader account-level object replication needed for a complete failover group requires Business Critical.

options.text: Any edition supports full security object replication
options.explanation: is incorrect. Not all editions support full security object replication. The feature matrix in the replication documentation clearly shows Business Critical as the minimum required edition for most account-level objects.

questions.stem: What is the effect of promoting a secondary account to primary status in a failover group?

options.text: The original primary account is permanently deleted
options.explanation: is incorrect. The original primary account is not deleted during failover. It continues to exist and becomes a secondary account in the failover group.

options.text: The secondary account gains read-write access to the replicated objects and the original primary becomes a secondary
options.explanation: is correct. Failover (promotion of a secondary to primary) is a controlled role-swap: the secondary account gains full read-write access to the replicated objects and becomes the new primary. The original primary is demoted to secondary status and switches to read-only mode for those objects. Replication then flows from the new primary to the old.

options.text: The secondary account creates a full independent copy and both accounts become primary simultaneously
options.explanation: is incorrect. Only one account in a failover group can be primary at any given time. Having two simultaneous primaries would create data conflicts, which is exactly what the failover model is designed to prevent.

options.text: Replication of the failover group is permanently disabled to prevent data conflicts
options.explanation: is incorrect. After failover, replication continues in the reverse direction (new primary to new secondary). Disabling replication permanently would defeat the purpose of maintaining a recovery standby.

questions.stem: Which SHOW command lists all accounts within the current organization when executed with ORGADMIN?

options.text: SHOW DATABASES
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW DATABASES lists databases within the current account. It has no awareness of other accounts in the organization.

options.text: SHOW ACCOUNTS
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW ACCOUNTS, when executed while using the ORGADMIN role, lists all accounts in the organization along with their region, edition, and other account-level properties.

options.text: SHOW ORGANIZATIONS
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW ORGANIZATIONS is not a valid Snowflake SQL command. The organization itself is managed through ORGADMIN-scoped commands and ORGANIZATION_USAGE views.

options.text: SHOW SHARES
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW SHARES lists data shares (outbound and inbound) for the current account. It does not list accounts in the organization.
